nodes.name: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
2D Gaussian Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Radiance Fields
MVSplat: Efficient 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Multi-View Images
Scaffold-GS: Structured 3D Gaussians for View-Adaptive Rendering

reasoning: W18 already covered Scaffold-GS lineage; the corrective + sparse-view branch is the freshest material. Pull bodies for Revising Densification, MVSplat, latentSplat, plus 2DGS for the geometry angle.
content: Eight matches. W18 already covered the SDS / LRM lines, so the genuinely fresh material is the corrective and feed-forward-from-sparse-views branch. Reading four bodies.

content.title: Revising Densification in Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Bulò et al. revisit the clone/split/prune schedule from the original 3DGS recipe and show that the criterion that triggers primitive cloning is undertuned. They propose a principled per-primitive criterion based on the variance of position gradients across views, plus a recalibrated opacity-reset schedule, and report a meaningful PSNR gain at equal or lower primitive count on Mip-NeRF 360 and Tanks-and-Temples...

content.title: MVSplat: Efficient 3D Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Multi-View Images
content.body: Chen et al. propose MVSplat, a feed-forward network that consumes a handful of posed multi-view images and directly predicts a set of 3D Gaussians, sidestepping per-scene optimisation. The architecture builds a cost volume on a plane sweep, decodes per-view depth, unprojects each pixel into a Gaussian primitive, and refines them with cross-view attention...

content.title: latentSplat: Autoencoding Variational Gaussians for Fast Generalizable 3D Reconstruction
content.body: Wewer et al. frame sparse-view reconstruction as variational inference over Gaussian parameters. A VAE encoder produces a latent posterior over Gaussian fields conditioned on the input views; sampling and decoding gives a coherent splat field that renders in real time...

content.title: 2D Gaussian Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Radiance Fields
content.body: Huang et al. collapse each 3D volumetric primitive to a flat, oriented 2D Gaussian disk so the geometry implied by the primitive cloud is view-consistent. A perspective-correct rasteriser keeps runtime close to 3DGS, and two regularisers (depth distortion, normal consistency) make the resulting meshes competitive with NeuS-style implicit-surface methods...

neighbors.name: How robust is multi-view consistency under fast camera motion?

reasoning: Good — MVSplat edges back to LGM and the multi-view-consistency question. That's the through-line: feed-forward GS still inherits the same multi-view robustness gap.
content: MVSplat and latentSplat are the headline. Both replace per-scene optimisation with a feed-forward predictor, and both still hit the same multi-view-consistency open question MonoGS does. That's the digest spine.

Hold on — before you write it up, the framing reads like a press release. The W18 synthesis already over-pitched LGM and that landed badly. Pull the 3DGS-SLAM thread tighter instead: MonoGS, GS-SLAM, and Splatting-SLAM all hit the same wall and that's a real story. Drop LGM — it's a 2024-02 paper, that was W18's beat.

reasoning: Fair pushback. LGM was indeed W18 and I was about to recycle it. The SLAM thread is the right tightening — MonoGS and GS-SLAM both surface the multi-view-consistency open question and Splatting-SLAM is the third leg I haven't read yet this week. Let me pull them.
content: Agreed — LGM belongs in W18, not W19. Let me re-read the SLAM trio properly before I draft.

content.title: GS-SLAM: Dense Visual SLAM with 3D Gaussian Splatting
content.body: Yan et al. propose GS-SLAM, a dense RGB-D SLAM front-end that uses a 3DGS map as the sole scene representation. Tracking is implemented as differentiable splat rendering against the live frame; mapping interleaves densification and pruning under a coarse-to-fine schedule. Evaluated on Replica and ScanNet; reports rendering quality competitive with iMAP / NICE-SLAM at ~8 FPS tracking. No EuRoC / TartanAir results — the trajectories are all well-behaved.

neighbors.name: How robust is multi-view consistency under fast camera motion?

reasoning: GS-SLAM is the third leg. All three SLAM papers (MonoGS, GS-SLAM, plus Splatting-SLAM is the genus — MonoGS is the canonical citation) evaluate only on Replica/TUM/ScanNet — well-behaved trajectories. None has reported on EuRoC/TartanAir where DROID-SLAM is the tracking baseline. That's the through-line: GS-as-SLAM-map is the architectural commitment, the fast-motion gap is the shared liability.
content: OK, the SLAM trio is MonoGS (Matsuki) + GS-SLAM (Yan). Both swap iMAP/NICE-SLAM's MLP map for a splat map, both report 3-8 FPS, both evaluate on benign trajectories only. The shared liability is DROID-SLAM territory — EuRoC / TartanAir — where neither has shown numbers. That's the tighter story.
Better. Where's the open question? You can't ship a weekly digest without one — that's the whole point of the format.

reasoning: Right. The MonoGS node_context already surfaced OpenQuestion:multi-view-consistency. That IS the question. Let me pull its body so I can summarise the W19 angle in one sentence.
content: It's right there in the MonoGS neighbour set — multi-view-consistency under fast camera motion. Let me pull the question doc to reuse the framing.

content.title: How robust is multi-view consistency under fast camera motion?
content.body: 3D Gaussian Splatting and its SLAM descendants assume reasonably smooth camera trajectories. MonoGS and GS-SLAM report tracking accuracy on Replica/TUM-RGBD but those datasets have well-behaved trajectories. DROID-SLAM is the tracking-accuracy reference on EuRoC/TartanAir where aggressive motion is part of the protocol. 2DGS attacks the *static* version of the property by collapsing primitives to view-consistent disks. The dynamic-motion version remains open.

reasoning: I have everything: SLAM trio tightened, LGM dropped, open question surfaced and tied to the SLAM thread. Write the corrected synthesis.
content: Got it. Drafting the corrected synthesis now — tighter SLAM thread, no LGM, multi-view-consistency under fast motion as the explicit open question.

# Weekly synthesis - 2026-W19

W18 closed on the feed-forward vs per-scene fork (LGM was the headline
there). W19 sharpens the SLAM thread: two Gaussian-Splatting SLAM
front-ends pulled into the corpus this week share the same architectural
commitment and the same blind spot. The geometry and corrective threads
moved too, but the SLAM trio is the story.

## The Splatting-SLAM trio converges on one design

[MonoGS](../../papers/arxiv-2312-06741/paper.md) (Matsuki et al., 2023) and
[GS-SLAM](../../papers/arxiv-2311-11700/paper.md) (Yan et al., 2023) replace
the iMAP/NICE-SLAM neural-field map with a
[3DGS](../../papers/arxiv-2308-04079/paper.md) splat field as the sole
scene representation. Tracking is differentiable splat rendering against
the live frame; mapping interleaves the canonical clone/split/prune
schedule. Both report 3-8 FPS, both run on benign captures
(Replica, TUM-RGBD, ScanNet). The architectural bet is clear: if 3DGS is
the right map representation for offline novel-view synthesis, it should
be the right map for live SLAM too.

## The geometry and corrective threads keep moving

[2D Gaussian Splatting](../../papers/arxiv-2403-17888/paper.md) (Huang et al.,
2024) collapses each primitive to a view-consistent disk; the implied
geometry is correct from every camera by construction.
[Revising Densification](../../papers/arxiv-2404-06109/paper.md)
(Bulo et al., 2024) is the quietly important paper: the original 3DGS
clone/split criterion was undertuned, and a variance-of-gradient
replacement gives meaningful PSNR at equal primitive count. Every
downstream method - including the SLAM trio - inherits the original
schedule and should be re-evaluated.

## The feed-forward branch, briefly

[MVSplat](../../papers/arxiv-2403-14627/paper.md) and
[latentSplat](../../papers/arxiv-2403-16292/paper.md) both predict a
Gaussian field from sparse posed views in a single forward pass.
Promising, but already covered as the W18 angle - flagged here only
because both surface the same open question the SLAM trio does.

## Open question: multi-view consistency under fast motion

All three SLAM papers - MonoGS, GS-SLAM, and the original 3DGS itself -
evaluate exclusively on quasi-static or well-behaved trajectories.
[DROID-SLAM](../../papers/arxiv-2108-10869/paper.md) remains the tracking
reference on EuRoC and TartanAir where aggressive motion is part of
the protocol, and no Splatting-SLAM paper has shown numbers on those
benchmarks. The
[open question](../../questions/multi-view-consistency.md) is whether
the GS map representation can survive that regime at all, or whether a
hybrid (DROID-SLAM tracking + GS map) is the actual production design.
Until someone runs the experiment, the architectural commitment is a bet,
not a conclusion.

## What to read next

For W20: any paper that evaluates a Splatting-SLAM front-end on
EuRoC or TartanAir, or any ablation that swaps DROID-SLAM's tracker
into MonoGS's mapping loop. If neither shows up, the question stays
open another week.

content: W19 digest drafted with the SLAM-thread tightening and LGM removed. Anchors the 3DGS base, two SLAM front-ends (MonoGS, GS-SLAM), 2DGS, Revising Densification, and the two feed-forward papers as supporting context. Multi-view-consistency under fast motion is the named open question.
